title: COVID-19 — Acute Management
oneLiner: Acute respiratory viral illness caused by SARS-CoV-2, ranging from mild upper respiratory symptoms to severe viral pneumonia and ARDS; global pandemic with high morbidity and mortality.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 72-year-old man presents with a 5-day history of dry cough, subjective fevers, and progressive shortness of breath. His oxygen saturations on room air are 88%, respiratory rate 28, heart rate 105, BP 110/70, temperature 38.4°C. Chest auscultation reveals bilateral fine crackles at the bases. His son mentions he’s been more confused over the past 24 hours. He has hypertension and type 2 diabetes. CXR shows bilateral peripheral ground-glass opacities. PCR from a nasopharyngeal swab returns positive for SARS-CoV-2.

**Dissecting the Vignette:**

| Pattern Clue | What It Tells You |
|--------------|-------------------|
| Age >65, hypertension, diabetes | High-risk for severe disease (older age, comorbidities) |
| Dry cough + fever + dyspnoea | Classic triad; suggests lower respiratory involvement |
| Sat 88% on air, RR 28 | Hypoxaemic respiratory failure; requires oxygen supplementation |
| Bilateral crackles on auscultation | Indicates alveolar filling process (viral pneumonia) |
| Confusion | Possible hypoxia-driven delirium or cerebral involvement; also a red flag for critical illness |
| Bilateral peripheral ground-glass opacities | Classic COVID-19 pneumonia pattern (viral interstitial pneumonia) |

**Atypical Presentations:**
- **"Happy hypoxia"** – A 55-year-old woman with saturations 80% but speaking full sentences, only mild dyspnoea. Danger: silent hypoxaemia delays escalation.
- **Gastrointestinal-only** – Young adult with nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, no respiratory symptoms; still COVID-19 positive.
- **Thromboembolic presentation** – Sudden-onset pleuritic chest pain + haemoptysis with no fever (pulmonary embolism as first manifestation of COVID-19-associated coagulopathy).

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
COVID-19 is an infectious disease caused by the SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus. It primarily targets the respiratory tract but can affect multiple organ systems via direct viral injury, immune dysregulation, and microvascular thrombosis.

**Why it matters:**  
- High incidence and transmissibility: still a common cause of hospital admission.  
- Rapid deterioration possible: "silent hypoxia" can mask severity until late.  
- Multisystem complications: ARDS, myocarditis, stroke, venous thromboembolism, renal failure.  
- Key management pitfalls: overuse of antibiotics, underuse of anticoagulation, delayed steroids.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Story of disease development:**  
SARS-CoV-2 enters respiratory epithelial cells via ACE2 receptors. Initial viral replication in upper airways causes mild symptoms (cough, sore throat). In severe cases, virus spreads to lower airways → direct alveolar epithelial damage + activation of innate immune response. This triggers a cytokine storm (IL-6, TNF-α, etc.) leading to diffuse alveolar damage, exudative phase of ARDS. Concurrently, endothelial injury activates coagulation cascade → microthrombi in pulmonary vasculature worsening V/Q mismatch. Later, fibrotic phase may cause persistent hypoxia.

**Mechanism → Sign:**

| Mechanism | Clinical Sign |
|-----------|---------------|
| Alveolar epithelial injury + fluid exudation | Crackles, ground-glass opacities on CXR/CT |
| V/Q mismatch from microthrombi | Hypoxaemia out of proportion to lung compliance ("happy hypoxia") |
| Cytokine storm → increased capillary permeability | Pulmonary oedema, ARDS |
| Endothelial activation → thrombotic microangiopathy | Elevated D-dimer, high VTE risk, acro-ischaemia (COVID toes) |
| ACE2 downregulation → relative angiotensin II excess | Hypertension exacerbation, myocardial injury |
| Immune-mediated myocardial inflammation | Raised troponin, arrhythmias |

**Connecting to Pattern Recognition:**  
- Bilateral peripheral opacities → virus preferentially infects type II pneumocytes in subpleural regions.  
- Hypoxaemia with relatively preserved lung compliance → microthrombi cause dead-space ventilation.  
- Confusion in elderly → cerebral hypoperfusion from hypoxia or microvascular thrombosis.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Cause | 1-line Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|------------------|
| **Infectious** | SARS-CoV-2 (most common) | Exposure to a known case or high-prevalence area |
| **Viral variants** | Alpha, Delta, Omicron subvariants | Different severity and symptom profile (Omicron more upper respiratory) |
| **Reinfection** | After prior natural infection or vaccination | Second episode with different variant, often milder |
| **Post-vaccination breakthrough** | Immunocompromised host | Onset >14 days after vaccination despite full course |

**Most common cause:** SARS-CoV-2 infection, transmitted via respiratory droplets and aerosols.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**
- **WHO definition:** Suspected case = acute onset of fever AND cough OR loss of taste/smell; OR severe respiratory illness with no other cause.
- **Confirmed:** SARS-CoV-2 detected by PCR (or rapid antigen test with clinical correlation).
- **Severity classification (NICE/WHO):**  
  - Mild: no oxygen requirement.  
  - Moderate: oxygen saturation <94% on air.  
  - Severe: SpO2 <90% or RR >30 or signs of pneumonia.  
  - Critical: ARDS, sepsis, shock.

**Key investigations:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| Nasopharyngeal swab PCR | Viral RNA | Confirm diagnosis | Positive = COVID-19 (false negative possible early/late) |
| CXR | Bilateral peripheral opacities | Assess pneumonia severity | Normal does not rule out; rapid progression to white-out in severe |
| Full blood count | Lymphopenia, mild thrombocytopenia | Prognostic marker | Lymphocyte count <1.0 ×10⁹/L associated with severe disease |
| CRP, ferritin, LDH | Elevated in cytokine storm | Markers of inflammation and severity | CRP >100 mg/L, ferritin >1000 µg/L, LDH >300 U/L → high risk |
| D-dimer | Elevated in thrombosis/coagulopathy | VTE risk stratification | >1000 ng/mL associated with increased mortality |
| Troponin | Elevated in myocardial injury | Screen for myocarditis | Any elevation = worse prognosis |
| Blood gases | PaO2, PaCO2, pH | Assess oxygenation and respiratory failure | PaO2/FiO2 ratio <300 = acute respiratory failure |

**Pattern-recognition:**  
- **Single finding that confirms:** Positive SARS-CoV-2 PCR.  
- **Single finding that rules out:** Negative PCR with alternative diagnosis (but clinical suspicion may warrant repeat).

---
sections.Immediate (first hour):
| Action | Why |
|--------|-----|
| **Assess severity: NEWS2, SpO2, respiratory rate, conscious level** | Triage to appropriate isolation and monitoring |
| **Supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula or mask** | Target SpO2 92-96% (88-92% if chronic hypercapnic) |
| **Check bloods + CXR + PCR** | Confirm diagnosis, stratify risk |
| **Empiric antibiotics if pneumonia suspected** | Community-acquired pneumonia overlap (e.g., doxycycline 200mg PO stat then 100mg OD for 5 days OR co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS PO/IV 5 days) |
| **IV fluids cautiously** | Avoid fluid overload in ARDS; give 250-500mL bolus only if hypotensive |
| **Thromboprophylaxis** | Enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if weight >100kg, 60mg) — unless high bleeding risk |
sections.Ward (24-72 hours):
| Action | Dose & Route | Why |
|--------|--------------|-----|
| **Dexamethasone** | 6mg PO/IV OD for 10 days (or until discharge) | Reduces mortality in oxygen-dependent patients (RECOVERY trial) |
| **Remdesivir** | 200mg IV day 1, then 100mg IV OD for 4 days | Indicated in hospitalised patients needing low-flow oxygen (if within 7 days of symptom onset) |
| **Anticoagulation** | Enoxaparin 1mg/kg SC BD (treatment dose) if high D-dimer or clinical suspicion of VTE | COVID-19 associated coagulopathy: prophylactic dose insufficient in some severe cases |
| **Proning (awake)** | Patient prone for 16h/day if conscious | Improves oxygenation by recruiting dependent lung |
| **Monitor fluid balance** | Strict I/O, avoid positive balance | ARDS management: conservative fluid strategy |

**What NOT to do:**
- Do not start **hydroxychloroquine** or **azithromycin** (no proven benefit).  
- Do not give **high-dose corticosteroids** (>6mg dexamethasone) routinely — increases harm.  
- Do not use **antibiotics** unless bacterial co-infection proven (low rates).  
- Do not **withhold anticoagulation** due to mild thrombocytopenia (unless <50 ×10⁹/L).
sections.Long-term (post-discharge):
| Action | Why |
|--------|-----|
| **Pulmonary rehabilitation** | COVID-19 survivors often have persistent dyspnoea, reduced exercise tolerance |
| **VTE prophylaxis** for 6 weeks (e.g., rivaroxaban 10mg OD) if high risk (DVT/PE in hospital, D-dimer >2000, obesity) | Post-discharge VTE risk elevated |
| **Monitor for long COVID** | Fatigue, brain fog, anosmia, cough — refer to post-COVID clinic |
| **Vaccination** | Offer full schedule (including boosters) at least 4 weeks after recovery |

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | COVID-19 | Influenza | Bacterial Pneumonia (e.g., Strep. pneumoniae) | Pneumocystis jirovecii (PCP) |
|---------|----------|-----------|-----------------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Onset** | Gradual over 3-7 days | Abrupt (hours) | Acute (1-3 days) | Subacute (weeks) |
| **Prodrome** | Fever, dry cough, anosmia | Fever, myalgia, headache | Fever, productive cough, pleuritic pain | Fever, dry cough, breathlessness |
| **Cough** | Dry | Dry initially | Productive (rusty sputum) | Dry |
| **Oxygenation** | Hypoxaemia often silent | Hypoxaemia possible | Hypoxaemia proportional to consolidation | Severe hypoxaemia |
| **CXR pattern** | Bilateral peripheral ground-glass | Bilateral interstitial or normal | Lobar consolidation | Bilateral perihilar interstitial |
| **Labs** | Lymphopenia, elevated CRP/ferritin/D-dimer | Lymphopenia, normal CRP often | Neutrophilia, high CRP | LDH >500, CD4 count <200 |
| **Key diagnostic test** | SARS-CoV-2 PCR | Influenza PCR | Sputum culture, urine antigen | Induced sputum PCR for PCP |
| **Treatment** | Supportive + dexamethasone ± remdesivir | Oseltamivir (if early <48h) | Antibiotics (amoxicillin, macrolide) | Co-trimoxazole + steroids |

**Pattern-recognition to differentiate:**
- Anosmia/ageusia is highly specific for COVID-19 (not in bacterial or influenza).  
- Abrupt onset with high fever and myalgia → influenza.  
- Lobar consolidation with neutrophils → bacterial.  
- Insidious onset in immunocompromised (HIV, transplant) → PCP.

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What To Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)** | Worsening hypoxia despite high-flow O2, respiratory distress | PaO2/FiO2 <200, bilateral opacities, low compliance | Escalate to ICU: prone ventilation, lung-protective ventilation, consider ECMO |
| **Pulmonary Embolism** | Sudden pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, tachycardia, hypoxia | ECG: S1Q3T3, CXR: normal or wedge infarct, D-dimer >5000 | CT pulmonary angiogram; treat with heparin infusion |
| **Myocarditis** | Chest pain, dyspnoea, arrhythmias | Elevated troponin, ECG: diffuse ST elevation, echo: reduced LVEF | Cardiology consult; treat as per myocarditis (ACEi, beta-blocker, supportive) |
| **Secondary Bacterial Pneumonia** | Worsening after initial improvement, purulent sputum, rigors | New infiltrate on CXR, raised WCC | Sputum culture, antibiotics (co-amoxiclav, levofloxacin) |
| **Cytokine Storm / Macrophage Activation Syndrome** | High fever, multiorgan failure, coagulopathy | Ferritin >10,000, CRP rising, sIL-2R high, haemophagocytosis on bone marrow | Treat with tocilizumab or baricitinib (after senior review) |
| **Acute Kidney Injury** | Oliguria, rising creatinine | Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h, creatinine >1.5x baseline | Fluid challenge, avoid nephrotoxins, consider RRT |
| **Long COVID (Post-COVID Syndrome)** | Fatigue, breathlessness, cognitive impairment >12 weeks post-infection | Normal investigations but persistent symptoms | Refer to post-COVID clinic, physiotherapy, psychological support |

**Late-presentation patterns:**  
- If missed early, COVID-19 can progress to "white-out" lung within 72 hours → high mortality.  
- Untreated hypoxaemia leads to cardiac arrest (hypoxic).  
- Missed PE can cause sudden death.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **COVID SCORE** (severity stratification — not a formal tool but memory aid)  
   - C – Comorbidities (diabetes, CVD)  
   - O – Oxygen saturation (<94%)  
   - V – Viral load (PCR cycle threshold low)  
   - I – Inflammatory markers (CRP >100, D-dimer >1000)  
   - D – Dyspnoea (RR >24)  

2. **"Don't Forget The D's"** (management pitfalls)  
   - **D**examethasone – but only if needing oxygen  
   - **D**oxycycline – if bacterial co-infection suspected  
   - **D**-dimer – drives anticoagulation  
   - **D**eath from hypoxia – prone and escalate  

3. **"ACE" for Pathophysiology**  
   - **A**lveolar injury (direct viral)  
   - **C**ytokine storm (immune)  
   - **E**ndothelial thrombosis (coagulopathy)

**High-Yield Clinical Pearls:**

1. "Happy hypoxia" is real: a patient with SpO2 80% may appear comfortable – check saturations on all admissions.
2. Dexamethasone 6mg is the standard dose – higher doses increase mortality.
3. Remdesivir only effective if started within 7 days of symptoms.
4. Prophylactic anticoagulation is mandatory; consider treatment dose for severe disease with high D-dimer.
5. Awake proning improves oxygenation – instruct patient to lie prone for as long as tolerated.
6. Do not withhold steroids for fear of immunosuppression – benefit outweighs risk.
7. Post-discharge VTE prophylaxis for 6 weeks reduces re-admissions with PE.

**Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
- Using antibiotics in all patients (bacterial co-infection is <10%).
- Starting high-flow oxygen without escalation plan – if FiO2 >0.4 needed, consult ICU.
- Forgetting to check D-dimer and not anticoagulating accordingly.
- Halting steroids if patient improves – complete 10-day course.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| When to Call | Specific Triggers | To Whom |
|--------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Deteriorating respiratory status** | SpO2 <90% despite 15L O2 via non-rebreather, RR >30, or PaO2/FiO2 <200 | Medical senior (Registrar/Consultant) |
| **High-risk features** | NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >4 mmol/L, GCS <15 or <3 point drop | ICU outreach team |
| **Cardiorespiratory instability** | Systolic BP <90 mmHg, heart rate >130, new arrhythmia | Crash team / medical emergency team |
| **Acute confusion / reduced GCS** | GCS ≤12, new focal neurology (stroke/TIA) | Neurology/ICU consult |
| **Rapidly rising troponin** | >99th percentile with ECG changes | Cardiology consult |
| **Bleeding on anticoagulation** | Major bleeding (Hb drop >2g/dL, GI bleed) | Senior + haematology |

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥5** – urgent medical review.
- **NEWS2 ≥7** – immediate review, call outreach.
- **PaO2/FiO2 <300** – moderate ARDS, consider ICU.
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** with hypotension – sepsis/MODS.
- **GCS <15** – hypoxia or CNS involvement.
- **D-dimer >3000 ng/mL** – high risk of VTE, consider treatment dose anticoagulation and CT-PA.

*Always escalate early in COVID-19: the window for intervention is narrow.*

rawMarkdown:
# COVID-19 — Acute Management

**One-liner:** Acute respiratory viral illness caused by SARS-CoV-2, ranging from mild upper respiratory symptoms to severe viral pneumonia and ARDS; global pandemic with high morbidity and mortality.

---

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**  
A 72-year-old man presents with a 5-day history of dry cough, subjective fevers, and progressive shortness of breath. His oxygen saturations on room air are 88%, respiratory rate 28, heart rate 105, BP 110/70, temperature 38.4°C. Chest auscultation reveals bilateral fine crackles at the bases. His son mentions he’s been more confused over the past 24 hours. He has hypertension and type 2 diabetes. CXR shows bilateral peripheral ground-glass opacities. PCR from a nasopharyngeal swab returns positive for SARS-CoV-2.

**Dissecting the Vignette:**

| Pattern Clue | What It Tells You |
|--------------|-------------------|
| Age >65, hypertension, diabetes | High-risk for severe disease (older age, comorbidities) |
| Dry cough + fever + dyspnoea | Classic triad; suggests lower respiratory involvement |
| Sat 88% on air, RR 28 | Hypoxaemic respiratory failure; requires oxygen supplementation |
| Bilateral crackles on auscultation | Indicates alveolar filling process (viral pneumonia) |
| Confusion | Possible hypoxia-driven delirium or cerebral involvement; also a red flag for critical illness |
| Bilateral peripheral ground-glass opacities | Classic COVID-19 pneumonia pattern (viral interstitial pneumonia) |

**Atypical Presentations:**
- **"Happy hypoxia"** – A 55-year-old woman with saturations 80% but speaking full sentences, only mild dyspnoea. Danger: silent hypoxaemia delays escalation.
- **Gastrointestinal-only** – Young adult with nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, no respiratory symptoms; still COVID-19 positive.
- **Thromboembolic presentation** – Sudden-onset pleuritic chest pain + haemoptysis with no fever (pulmonary embolism as first manifestation of COVID-19-associated coagulopathy).

---

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
COVID-19 is an infectious disease caused by the SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus. It primarily targets the respiratory tract but can affect multiple organ systems via direct viral injury, immune dysregulation, and microvascular thrombosis.

**Why it matters:**  
- High incidence and transmissibility: still a common cause of hospital admission.  
- Rapid deterioration possible: "silent hypoxia" can mask severity until late.  
- Multisystem complications: ARDS, myocarditis, stroke, venous thromboembolism, renal failure.  
- Key management pitfalls: overuse of antibiotics, underuse of anticoagulation, delayed steroids.

---

## 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**Story of disease development:**  
SARS-CoV-2 enters respiratory epithelial cells via ACE2 receptors. Initial viral replication in upper airways causes mild symptoms (cough, sore throat). In severe cases, virus spreads to lower airways → direct alveolar epithelial damage + activation of innate immune response. This triggers a cytokine storm (IL-6, TNF-α, etc.) leading to diffuse alveolar damage, exudative phase of ARDS. Concurrently, endothelial injury activates coagulation cascade → microthrombi in pulmonary vasculature worsening V/Q mismatch. Later, fibrotic phase may cause persistent hypoxia.

**Mechanism → Sign:**

| Mechanism | Clinical Sign |
|-----------|---------------|
| Alveolar epithelial injury + fluid exudation | Crackles, ground-glass opacities on CXR/CT |
| V/Q mismatch from microthrombi | Hypoxaemia out of proportion to lung compliance ("happy hypoxia") |
| Cytokine storm → increased capillary permeability | Pulmonary oedema, ARDS |
| Endothelial activation → thrombotic microangiopathy | Elevated D-dimer, high VTE risk, acro-ischaemia (COVID toes) |
| ACE2 downregulation → relative angiotensin II excess | Hypertension exacerbation, myocardial injury |
| Immune-mediated myocardial inflammation | Raised troponin, arrhythmias |

**Connecting to Pattern Recognition:**  
- Bilateral peripheral opacities → virus preferentially infects type II pneumocytes in subpleural regions.  
- Hypoxaemia with relatively preserved lung compliance → microthrombi cause dead-space ventilation.  
- Confusion in elderly → cerebral hypoperfusion from hypoxia or microvascular thrombosis.

---

## 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Cause | 1-line Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|------------------|
| **Infectious** | SARS-CoV-2 (most common) | Exposure to a known case or high-prevalence area |
| **Viral variants** | Alpha, Delta, Omicron subvariants | Different severity and symptom profile (Omicron more upper respiratory) |
| **Reinfection** | After prior natural infection or vaccination | Second episode with different variant, often milder |
| **Post-vaccination breakthrough** | Immunocompromised host | Onset >14 days after vaccination despite full course |

**Most common cause:** SARS-CoV-2 infection, transmitted via respiratory droplets and aerosols.

---

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**
- **WHO definition:** Suspected case = acute onset of fever AND cough OR loss of taste/smell; OR severe respiratory illness with no other cause.
- **Confirmed:** SARS-CoV-2 detected by PCR (or rapid antigen test with clinical correlation).
- **Severity classification (NICE/WHO):**  
  - Mild: no oxygen requirement.  
  - Moderate: oxygen saturation <94% on air.  
  - Severe: SpO2 <90% or RR >30 or signs of pneumonia.  
  - Critical: ARDS, sepsis, shock.

**Key investigations:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| Nasopharyngeal swab PCR | Viral RNA | Confirm diagnosis | Positive = COVID-19 (false negative possible early/late) |
| CXR | Bilateral peripheral opacities | Assess pneumonia severity | Normal does not rule out; rapid progression to white-out in severe |
| Full blood count | Lymphopenia, mild thrombocytopenia | Prognostic marker | Lymphocyte count <1.0 ×10⁹/L associated with severe disease |
| CRP, ferritin, LDH | Elevated in cytokine storm | Markers of inflammation and severity | CRP >100 mg/L, ferritin >1000 µg/L, LDH >300 U/L → high risk |
| D-dimer | Elevated in thrombosis/coagulopathy | VTE risk stratification | >1000 ng/mL associated with increased mortality |
| Troponin | Elevated in myocardial injury | Screen for myocarditis | Any elevation = worse prognosis |
| Blood gases | PaO2, PaCO2, pH | Assess oxygenation and respiratory failure | PaO2/FiO2 ratio <300 = acute respiratory failure |

**Pattern-recognition:**  
- **Single finding that confirms:** Positive SARS-CoV-2 PCR.  
- **Single finding that rules out:** Negative PCR with alternative diagnosis (but clinical suspicion may warrant repeat).

---

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

### Immediate (first hour)

| Action | Why |
|--------|-----|
| **Assess severity: NEWS2, SpO2, respiratory rate, conscious level** | Triage to appropriate isolation and monitoring |
| **Supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula or mask** | Target SpO2 92-96% (88-92% if chronic hypercapnic) |
| **Check bloods + CXR + PCR** | Confirm diagnosis, stratify risk |
| **Empiric antibiotics if pneumonia suspected** | Community-acquired pneumonia overlap (e.g., doxycycline 200mg PO stat then 100mg OD for 5 days OR co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS PO/IV 5 days) |
| **IV fluids cautiously** | Avoid fluid overload in ARDS; give 250-500mL bolus only if hypotensive |
| **Thromboprophylaxis** | Enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (if weight >100kg, 60mg) — unless high bleeding risk |

### Ward (24-72 hours)

| Action | Dose & Route | Why |
|--------|--------------|-----|
| **Dexamethasone** | 6mg PO/IV OD for 10 days (or until discharge) | Reduces mortality in oxygen-dependent patients (RECOVERY trial) |
| **Remdesivir** | 200mg IV day 1, then 100mg IV OD for 4 days | Indicated in hospitalised patients needing low-flow oxygen (if within 7 days of symptom onset) |
| **Anticoagulation** | Enoxaparin 1mg/kg SC BD (treatment dose) if high D-dimer or clinical suspicion of VTE | COVID-19 associated coagulopathy: prophylactic dose insufficient in some severe cases |
| **Proning (awake)** | Patient prone for 16h/day if conscious | Improves oxygenation by recruiting dependent lung |
| **Monitor fluid balance** | Strict I/O, avoid positive balance | ARDS management: conservative fluid strategy |

**What NOT to do:**
- Do not start **hydroxychloroquine** or **azithromycin** (no proven benefit).  
- Do not give **high-dose corticosteroids** (>6mg dexamethasone) routinely — increases harm.  
- Do not use **antibiotics** unless bacterial co-infection proven (low rates).  
- Do not **withhold anticoagulation** due to mild thrombocytopenia (unless <50 ×10⁹/L).

### Long-term (post-discharge)

| Action | Why |
|--------|-----|
| **Pulmonary rehabilitation** | COVID-19 survivors often have persistent dyspnoea, reduced exercise tolerance |
| **VTE prophylaxis** for 6 weeks (e.g., rivaroxaban 10mg OD) if high risk (DVT/PE in hospital, D-dimer >2000, obesity) | Post-discharge VTE risk elevated |
| **Monitor for long COVID** | Fatigue, brain fog, anosmia, cough — refer to post-COVID clinic |
| **Vaccination** | Offer full schedule (including boosters) at least 4 weeks after recovery |

---

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | COVID-19 | Influenza | Bacterial Pneumonia (e.g., Strep. pneumoniae) | Pneumocystis jirovecii (PCP) |
|---------|----------|-----------|-----------------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Onset** | Gradual over 3-7 days | Abrupt (hours) | Acute (1-3 days) | Subacute (weeks) |
| **Prodrome** | Fever, dry cough, anosmia | Fever, myalgia, headache | Fever, productive cough, pleuritic pain | Fever, dry cough, breathlessness |
| **Cough** | Dry | Dry initially | Productive (rusty sputum) | Dry |
| **Oxygenation** | Hypoxaemia often silent | Hypoxaemia possible | Hypoxaemia proportional to consolidation | Severe hypoxaemia |
| **CXR pattern** | Bilateral peripheral ground-glass | Bilateral interstitial or normal | Lobar consolidation | Bilateral perihilar interstitial |
| **Labs** | Lymphopenia, elevated CRP/ferritin/D-dimer | Lymphopenia, normal CRP often | Neutrophilia, high CRP | LDH >500, CD4 count <200 |
| **Key diagnostic test** | SARS-CoV-2 PCR | Influenza PCR | Sputum culture, urine antigen | Induced sputum PCR for PCP |
| **Treatment** | Supportive + dexamethasone ± remdesivir | Oseltamivir (if early <48h) | Antibiotics (amoxicillin, macrolide) | Co-trimoxazole + steroids |

**Pattern-recognition to differentiate:**
- Anosmia/ageusia is highly specific for COVID-19 (not in bacterial or influenza).  
- Abrupt onset with high fever and myalgia → influenza.  
- Lobar consolidation with neutrophils → bacterial.  
- Insidious onset in immunocompromised (HIV, transplant) → PCP.

---

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How It Presents | Key Signs | What To Do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)** | Worsening hypoxia despite high-flow O2, respiratory distress | PaO2/FiO2 <200, bilateral opacities, low compliance | Escalate to ICU: prone ventilation, lung-protective ventilation, consider ECMO |
| **Pulmonary Embolism** | Sudden pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, tachycardia, hypoxia | ECG: S1Q3T3, CXR: normal or wedge infarct, D-dimer >5000 | CT pulmonary angiogram; treat with heparin infusion |
| **Myocarditis** | Chest pain, dyspnoea, arrhythmias | Elevated troponin, ECG: diffuse ST elevation, echo: reduced LVEF | Cardiology consult; treat as per myocarditis (ACEi, beta-blocker, supportive) |
| **Secondary Bacterial Pneumonia** | Worsening after initial improvement, purulent sputum, rigors | New infiltrate on CXR, raised WCC | Sputum culture, antibiotics (co-amoxiclav, levofloxacin) |
| **Cytokine Storm / Macrophage Activation Syndrome** | High fever, multiorgan failure, coagulopathy | Ferritin >10,000, CRP rising, sIL-2R high, haemophagocytosis on bone marrow | Treat with tocilizumab or baricitinib (after senior review) |
| **Acute Kidney Injury** | Oliguria, rising creatinine | Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h, creatinine >1.5x baseline | Fluid challenge, avoid nephrotoxins, consider RRT |
| **Long COVID (Post-COVID Syndrome)** | Fatigue, breathlessness, cognitive impairment >12 weeks post-infection | Normal investigations but persistent symptoms | Refer to post-COVID clinic, physiotherapy, psychological support |

**Late-presentation patterns:**  
- If missed early, COVID-19 can progress to "white-out" lung within 72 hours → high mortality.  
- Untreated hypoxaemia leads to cardiac arrest (hypoxic).  
- Missed PE can cause sudden death.

---

## 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **COVID SCORE** (severity stratification — not a formal tool but memory aid)  
   - C – Comorbidities (diabetes, CVD)  
   - O – Oxygen saturation (<94%)  
   - V – Viral load (PCR cycle threshold low)  
   - I – Inflammatory markers (CRP >100, D-dimer >1000)  
   - D – Dyspnoea (RR >24)  

2. **"Don't Forget The D's"** (management pitfalls)  
   - **D**examethasone – but only if needing oxygen  
   - **D**oxycycline – if bacterial co-infection suspected  
   - **D**-dimer – drives anticoagulation  
   - **D**eath from hypoxia – prone and escalate  

3. **"ACE" for Pathophysiology**  
   - **A**lveolar injury (direct viral)  
   - **C**ytokine storm (immune)  
   - **E**ndothelial thrombosis (coagulopathy)

**High-Yield Clinical Pearls:**

1. "Happy hypoxia" is real: a patient with SpO2 80% may appear comfortable – check saturations on all admissions.
2. Dexamethasone 6mg is the standard dose – higher doses increase mortality.
3. Remdesivir only effective if started within 7 days of symptoms.
4. Prophylactic anticoagulation is mandatory; consider treatment dose for severe disease with high D-dimer.
5. Awake proning improves oxygenation – instruct patient to lie prone for as long as tolerated.
6. Do not withhold steroids for fear of immunosuppression – benefit outweighs risk.
7. Post-discharge VTE prophylaxis for 6 weeks reduces re-admissions with PE.

**Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
- Using antibiotics in all patients (bacterial co-infection is <10%).
- Starting high-flow oxygen without escalation plan – if FiO2 >0.4 needed, consult ICU.
- Forgetting to check D-dimer and not anticoagulating accordingly.
- Halting steroids if patient improves – complete 10-day course.

---

## 10. Escalation Criteria

| When to Call | Specific Triggers | To Whom |
|--------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Deteriorating respiratory status** | SpO2 <90% despite 15L O2 via non-rebreather, RR >30, or PaO2/FiO2 <200 | Medical senior (Registrar/Consultant) |
| **High-risk features** | NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >4 mmol/L, GCS <15 or <3 point drop | ICU outreach team |
| **Cardiorespiratory instability** | Systolic BP <90 mmHg, heart rate >130, new arrhythmia | Crash team / medical emergency team |
| **Acute confusion / reduced GCS** | GCS ≤12, new focal neurology (stroke/TIA) | Neurology/ICU consult |
| **Rapidly rising troponin** | >99th percentile with ECG changes | Cardiology consult |
| **Bleeding on anticoagulation** | Major bleeding (Hb drop >2g/dL, GI bleed) | Senior + haematology |

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥5** – urgent medical review.
- **NEWS2 ≥7** – immediate review, call outreach.
- **PaO2/FiO2 <300** – moderate ARDS, consider ICU.
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** with hypotension – sepsis/MODS.
- **GCS <15** – hypoxia or CNS involvement.
- **D-dimer >3000 ng/mL** – high risk of VTE, consider treatment dose anticoagulation and CT-PA.

*Always escalate early in COVID-19: the window for intervention is narrow.*
